county_metadata.research_tips: County Clerk in Jonesboro holds records from 1818 — an original county at statehood. Jonesboro was the site of a Lincoln-Douglas debate (1858), the southernmost debate in Illinois. Union County is in the Shawnee Hills — Trail of Tears forced march passed through. Anna State Hospital (1869) records at Illinois State Archives. Check Johnson County for pre-1818 records. Union County Historical and Genealogical Society. IRAD at Southern Illinois University Carbondale. Cobden area fruit orchards. Upland South settlers.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1818. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Illinois deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — original patents from BLM/GLO.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival — continuous records from county formation

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses and returns from 1818. County Clerk issues licenses. Applications include ages, birthplaces, and parents' names (details improve post-1870s). Statewide marriage registration from 1962.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well-preserved at County Clerk office

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, guardianships, and estate inventories from 1818. Illinois Circuit Court handles probate matters. Estate inventories often list heirs and personal property in detail.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Probate records generally well-preserved

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil and criminal case files, naturalizations (pre-1906), and judgments from 1818. Illinois Circuit Court is the trial court of general jurisdiction. Early records include debt cases, divorce proceedings, and guardianship matters that identify family relationships.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Court records generally well-preserved at county level

source_profiles.repository: Union County Clerk, Jonesboro / Illinois State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Tax assessment rolls list property owners, land descriptions, and property values. County Clerk records from 1818. Early tax lists serve as census substitutes. Illinois State Archives holds some county tax records on microfilm.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Some tax records at Illinois State Archives on microfilm; IRAD holdings vary by county

source_profiles.source_name: BLM General Land Office Records — Union County
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management / Illinois State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents for Illinois. As a public-land state, original land entries were through federal land offices. Cash entry, credit, and preemption patents. Illinois State Archives also holds duplicate patent records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: BLM-GLO digitized — searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.research_tips: County Clerk in Danville holds records from 1826. Danville was an important industrial city and Abraham Lincoln's law practice base in eastern Illinois — Lincoln practiced at the Vermilion County courthouse. Salt works at the Vermilion Salines were an early industry. Coal mining drew immigrant workers. Vermilion County Museum Society. IRAD at the University of Illinois. Danville had a significant African American community. Check Edgar County for pre-1826 records. Strong railroad heritage — multiple lines crossed here.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1826. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Illinois deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — original patents from BLM/GLO.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival — continuous records from county formation

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses and returns from 1826. County Clerk issues licenses. Applications include ages, birthplaces, and parents' names (details improve post-1870s). Statewide marriage registration from 1962.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well-preserved at County Clerk office

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, guardianships, and estate inventories from 1826. Illinois Circuit Court handles probate matters. Estate inventories often list heirs and personal property in detail.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Probate records generally well-preserved

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil and criminal case files, naturalizations (pre-1906), and judgments from 1826. Illinois Circuit Court is the trial court of general jurisdiction. Early records include debt cases, divorce proceedings, and guardianship matters that identify family relationships.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Court records generally well-preserved at county level

source_profiles.repository: Vermilion County Clerk, Danville / Illinois State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Tax assessment rolls list property owners, land descriptions, and property values. County Clerk records from 1826. Early tax lists serve as census substitutes. Illinois State Archives holds some county tax records on microfilm.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Some tax records at Illinois State Archives on microfilm; IRAD holdings vary by county

source_profiles.source_name: BLM General Land Office Records — Vermilion County
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management / Illinois State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents for Illinois. As a public-land state, original land entries were through federal land offices. Cash entry, credit, and preemption patents. Illinois State Archives also holds duplicate patent records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: BLM-GLO digitized — searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.repository: Illinois State Archives / Illinois Department of Mines and Minerals
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Mine inspection reports, employment records, accident reports, and company records. Illinois coal mining drew workers from Italy, Lithuania, Poland, and other European countries as well as Appalachian migrants. Annual coal reports published by the State of Illinois list mines and operators.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Mine inspection records at Illinois State Archives; some company records in local collections

source_profiles.repository: Vermilion County Circuit Clerk, Danville / NARA Chicago
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Declarations of intention, petitions for naturalization, and certificates. Pre-1906 records at county Circuit Court. Post-1906 copies at NARA Great Lakes Region (Chicago). Illinois attracted massive European immigration — German, Irish, Swedish, Norwegian, Czech, Polish, Italian, Lithuanian, and other groups.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Many Illinois naturalization records indexed on FamilySearch and Ancestry

source_profiles.source_name: African American Church and Community Records — Vermilion County
source_profiles.repository: Vivian G. Harsh Research Collection (Chicago Public Library) / local churches and historical societies
source_profiles.coverage_notes: African American church records (AME, Baptist, COGIC), fraternal organization records, and community newspapers. The Great Migration (1910-1970) brought hundreds of thousands of African Americans from the South to Illinois, particularly Chicago and industrial cities. Chicago Defender newspaper is a critical source.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Vivian G. Harsh Collection at Chicago Public Library is the largest African American history collection in the Midwest

source_profiles.source_name: Eighth Judicial Circuit Court Records — Vermilion County
source_profiles.repository: Vermilion County Circuit Clerk, Danville / Illinois State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Abraham Lincoln practiced law on Illinois' Eighth Judicial Circuit from 1837 to 1860. Circuit court records from this period include cases Lincoln argued and contemporaries he worked with. Court records also document the legal, economic, and social history of central Illinois families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Circuit court records at county level; Lincoln Legal Papers project has compiled many Lincoln-related documents

county_metadata.research_tips: County Clerk in Mount Carmel holds records from 1824. Mount Carmel is on the Wabash River at the Indiana border — check Gibson County, Indiana for cross-border families. Wabash County attracted both Upland South and German settlers. Check Edwards County for pre-1824 records. Wabash County Historical Society. IRAD at Eastern Illinois University. Oil production in the 20th century generated land records. River commerce records along the Wabash. Strong Methodist and German Evangelical church records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1824. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Illinois deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — original patents from BLM/GLO.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival — continuous records from county formation

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses and returns from 1824. County Clerk issues licenses. Applications include ages, birthplaces, and parents' names (details improve post-1870s). Statewide marriage registration from 1962.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well-preserved at County Clerk office

source_profiles.repository: Wabash County Circuit Clerk, Mount Carmel
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, guardianships, and estate inventories from 1824. Illinois Circuit Court handles probate matters. Estate inventories often list heirs and personal property in detail.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Probate records generally well-preserved

source_profiles.repository: Wabash County Circuit Clerk, Mount Carmel
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil and criminal case files, naturalizations (pre-1906), and judgments from 1824. Illinois Circuit Court is the trial court of general jurisdiction. Early records include debt cases, divorce proceedings, and guardianship matters that identify family relationships.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Court records generally well-preserved at county level

source_profiles.repository: Wabash County Clerk, Mount Carmel / Illinois State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Tax assessment rolls list property owners, land descriptions, and property values. County Clerk records from 1824. Early tax lists serve as census substitutes. Illinois State Archives holds some county tax records on microfilm.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Some tax records at Illinois State Archives on microfilm; IRAD holdings vary by county

source_profiles.source_name: BLM General Land Office Records — Wabash County
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management / Illinois State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents for Illinois. As a public-land state, original land entries were through federal land offices. Cash entry, credit, and preemption patents. Illinois State Archives also holds duplicate patent records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: BLM-GLO digitized — searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.source_name: Oil and Gas Lease Records — Wabash County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Oil and gas lease agreements recorded in county deed books. Illinois oil fields (particularly in southeastern Illinois) generated extensive lease and mineral rights records from the early 1900s onward. These records identify landowners and track property transactions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well-preserved in county deed records

county_metadata.research_tips: County Clerk in Monmouth holds records from 1825. Monmouth College (1853) in Monmouth — college archives hold local materials. Warren County is in the Military Tract — land bounties for War of 1812 veterans. Monmouth was the birthplace of Wyatt Earp. Check Pike County for pre-1825 records. Warren County Historical Society. IRAD at Western Illinois University. Scottish and New England settlers. Strong Reformed Presbyterian (Covenanter) church records unique to the Monmouth area. Agricultural heritage.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1825. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Illinois deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — original patents from BLM/GLO.

source_profiles.survival_notes: Probate records generally well-preserved

source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management / Illinois State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents for Illinois. As a public-land state, original land entries were through federal land offices. Cash entry, credit, and preemption patents. Illinois State Archives also holds duplicate patent records.

source_profiles.source_name: Military Tract Bounty Land Records — Warren County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: The Military Tract between the Illinois and Mississippi Rivers was reserved for War of 1812 veterans' bounty land warrants. Many warrants were sold to speculators. Patent and warrant records identify original claimants and purchasers.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well-documented — patents at BLM-GLO; warrant records at NARA

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Declarations of intention, petitions for naturalization, and certificates. Pre-1906 records at county Circuit Court. source_profiles.survival_notes: Many Illinois naturalization records indexed on FamilySearch and Ancestry

county_metadata.research_tips: County Clerk in Nashville holds records from 1818 — an original county at statehood. Washington County is in southern-central Illinois. Nashville is a small county seat. Created from St. Clair — check St. Clair for earlier records. Washington County Historical Society. IRAD at Southern Illinois University Edwardsville. German Catholic community around Nashville — St. Ann's parish records important. Agricultural county with some coal mining heritage. Strong German and Upland South settlement mix.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1818. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Illinois deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — original patents from BLM/GLO.

source_profiles.repository: Diocese of Belleville / Diocese of Springfield / local parishes
source_profiles.coverage_notes: German Catholic and Lutheran parish records — baptisms, marriages, and burials. Many parishes kept records in Latin or German. Parish registers often include parents' birthplaces in Germany. German settlement in Illinois was extensive, particularly in southwestern and central Illinois.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Parish records generally well-preserved; some microfilmed by FamilySearch

county_metadata.research_tips: County Clerk in Fairfield holds records from 1819. Wayne County is in southeastern Illinois — oil production from the Wayne County oil fields was significant in the 20th century and generated extensive land lease records. Created from Edwards — check Edwards for pre-1819 records. Wayne County Historical Society. IRAD at Southern Illinois University Carbondale. Fairfield is the county seat. Early settlers from Kentucky and Tennessee. Strong Baptist and Methodist church records. Agricultural heritage.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1819. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Illinois deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — original patents from BLM/GLO.

Created from Gallatin — check Gallatin for pre-1815 records. White County Historical Society. IRAD at Southern Illinois University Carbondale. Oil production in the 20th century. Robinson-White County area oil field records. Early Upland South settlers from Kentucky, Tennessee, and North Carolina. Strong Baptist church records.

county_metadata.research_tips: County Clerk in Morrison holds records from 1836. Whiteside County is on the Rock River and Mississippi River. Prophetstown was named for Wabokieshiek (White Cloud), a Ho-Chunk prophet. Sterling-Rock Falls industrial heritage. Check Jo Daviess and Henry for pre-1836 records. Whiteside County Historical Society. IRAD at Northern Illinois University. Strong Swedish and German settlement. Northwestern Steel and Wire employment records document immigrant workers. Agricultural and industrial mix.

county_metadata.research_tips: County Clerk in Joliet holds records from 1836. Joliet is a major city south of Chicago — Illinois State Penitentiary at Joliet (Old Joliet Prison, 1858) records at Illinois State Archives. Illinois & Michigan Canal passed through — canal records document Irish workers. Check Cook and Iroquois for pre-1836 records (note Cook County 1871 fire). Will County Historical Society. Joliet was an industrial center — limestone quarrying and steel. IRAD at Northern Illinois University. Diverse immigrant communities.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1836. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Illinois deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — original patents from BLM/GLO.

source_profiles.source_name: Illinois & Michigan Canal Records — Will County
source_profiles.repository: Illinois State Archives / I&M Canal National Heritage Corridor
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Canal construction records, land sales, and worker rolls. The I&M Canal (1836-1848) connected Lake Michigan to the Illinois River. Canal construction employed thousands of Irish immigrant laborers. Canal land grant records document property sales along the corridor.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Canal Commission records at Illinois State Archives

county_metadata.research_tips: County Clerk in Marion holds records from 1839. Williamson County was a major coal mining center — the Herrin Massacre (1922) was a violent mine strike confrontation that killed 23. Mine employment records document Italian, Eastern European, and Appalachian migrant workers. Check Franklin County for pre-1839 records. Williamson County Historical Society. IRAD at Southern Illinois University Carbondale. Crab Orchard National Wildlife Refuge area. Strong labor union heritage.
